mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) reflects a strengthening labor market and robust aggregate demand, leading to higher expected corporate earnings and positive equity returns. This is a direct, always-on signal of economic health.
evidence_refs: fact:Post-2009 recovery has been characterized by steady job growth.
failure_condition: A positive 6-month change in PAYEMS is followed by negative S&P 500 20-day returns more than 50% of the time over a 5-year period.

mechanism_narrative: When year-over-year inflation (CPIAUCSL) accelerates past 3%, it signals potential overheating and prompts expectations of monetary tightening by the Federal Reserve. Higher discount rates and reduced corporate margins lead to negative equity returns.
failure_condition: In periods where YoY CPI is above 3%, the S&P 500 average 60-day forward return is not statistically significantly lower than in periods where it is below 3%.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous rise in unemployment (UNRATE) and a fall in industrial production (INDPRO) indicates a stagflationary shock. This combination of weakening demand and supply constraints is highly detrimental to corporate profitability, causing negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Stagflationary environment of the 1970s was bearish for equities.
failure_condition: The model's rank IC remains below 0.03 over any 3-year rolling window when this trigger condition is met.

mechanism_narrative: In a low interest rate environment (FEDFUNDS < 1%), accelerating housing starts (HOUST) signal strong consumer confidence and credit availability, driving a wealth effect and boosting sectors like construction and finance, leading to positive market returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Housing market recovery is a key component of the post-2008 economic recovery.
failure_condition: When FEDFUNDS is below 1%, the correlation between HOUST 3-month changes and S&P 500 20-day forward returns is negative over a 4-year period.

mechanism_narrative: Strong growth in retail sales (RSAFS) indicates robust consumer spending, which constitutes a large portion of GDP. This directly translates to higher corporate revenues and earnings expectations, driving positive short-term equity performance.
evidence_refs: fact:Consumer spending is approximately 70% of the US economy.
failure_condition: The information coefficient between the 6-month change in RSAFS and 20-day forward S&P 500 returns is consistently below 0.02.

mechanism_narrative: A rapid deterioration in the labor market, signaled by the unemployment rate (UNRATE) rising more than 0.5pp in 6 months (the 'Sahm Rule' precursor), indicates the onset of a recession. This triggers a flight to safety and broad equity market declines.
evidence_refs: fact:Sharp increases in unemployment have preceded every modern US recession.
failure_condition: In the 12 months following a trigger event, cumulative S&P 500 returns are positive in more than 40% of historical instances.

mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds rate is near the zero lower bound (ZLB), it provides maximum monetary accommodation, suppressing short-term yields and encouraging investment in riskier assets like equities. This effect supports positive returns over very short horizons.
evidence_refs: fact:The Fed has maintained a ZIRP policy since late 2008.
failure_condition: While FEDFUNDS < 0.25%, the average 5-day forward S&P 500 return is not statistically different from the average return when FEDFUNDS > 0.25%.

mechanism_narrative: When industrial production (INDPRO) growth outpaces real GDP (GDPC1) growth, it suggests a build-up of inventories and potential oversupply. This condition pressures corporate margins and signals a cyclical peak, leading to negative equity returns.
candidate_trigger: YoY % change INDPRO > YoY % change GDPC1
failure_condition: The S&P 500 produces positive 60-day returns in more than 60% of triggered instances despite the negative signal.
